Climate Change Impacts on the Built Environment
===============================================

Overview
--------

The [term "built environment"](https://www.epa.gov/smm/basic-information-about-built-environment)includes structures and systems that provide places for people to live, work, and play. It includes buildings, roads, bridges, parks, streets, and systems that provide transportation, water, power, and more. These structures and systems exist in nearly all places where people live and work, but tend to be most concentrated in metropolitan areas. About 85% of Americans live in metropolitan areas, and that number is projected to grow over the next 100 years.[1](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#1foot)

Did You Know?

slide 2 of 4

*   ![Image 4: Red haze over San Francisco](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/gallery_medium/private/images/2022-11/iStock-1282388644-v2.jpg?h=e2e579bd&itok=NTMcFWeh) 

**Reduced air quality.** In the West, wildfires are on the rise, affecting air quality in nearby cities while also posing risks to people and property.

*   ![Image 5: Street partially submerged in floodwater](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/gallery_medium/private/images/2022-11/iStock-601945516.jpg?h=28121b77&itok=WtfkJB94) 

**More urban flooding.** Intense, single-day rainstorms are rising, increasing the risk of urban flooding, especially in areas with a lot of paved surfaces.[35](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#35foot)

*   ![Image 6: Crowd of people in New York City](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/gallery_medium/private/images/2022-11/iStock-1148966895.jpg?h=0d37b6ac&itok=DLIxlZhr) 

**Increased heat impacts.** Many cities already experience urban heat islands. Now, unusually hot summer days and nights are also becoming more common.[36](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#36foot) Urban residents can face more heat illnesses, power outages, and higher energy prices as a result.

*   ![Image 7: Pastel row of buildings in Charleston, NC.](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/gallery_medium/private/images/2022-11/iStock-1149331732.jpg?h=df8e4ff4&itok=ySRH65ME) 

**Increased property damage.** Charleston, South Carolina, is at risk for coastal flooding and sea level rise, both of which can cause damage to historic buildings and landmarks. The city is elevating some buildings to protect them from climate change.

*   ![Image 8: Red haze over San Francisco](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/gallery_medium/private/images/2022-11/iStock-1282388644-v2.jpg?h=e2e579bd&itok=NTMcFWeh) 

**Reduced air quality.** In the West, wildfires are on the rise, affecting air quality in nearby cities while also posing risks to people and property.

*   ![Image 9: Street partially submerged in floodwater](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/gallery_medium/private/images/2022-11/iStock-601945516.jpg?h=28121b77&itok=WtfkJB94) 

**More urban flooding.** Intense, single-day rainstorms are rising, increasing the risk of urban flooding, especially in areas with a lot of paved surfaces.[35](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#35foot)

![Image 10: Green building with trees and shrubs on rooftop](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/small/private/images/2022-11/build-enviornment-greenroofs.jpg?itok=PoMuonHa)

Green roofs provide shade, remove heat from the air, and provide many other benefits in urban areas.

Climate change affects cities differently depending on their location. Many cities are already dealing with multiple impacts from climate change. For example, some experience more frequent and longer heat waves.[2](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#2foot) Those impacts worsen the temperature problems caused by [urban heat islands](https://www.epa.gov/heatislands)—that is, city areas with higher temperatures than outlying areas.[3](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#3foot) At the same time, some urban areas are experiencing repeated flooding from heavy rains that overwhelm drainage systems. Others face flooding problems due to losses of wetlands and forests that once absorbed rainfall.[4](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#4foot)

In addition, the built environment interacts with and depends on other sectors of the economy, such as [water](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-freshwater-resources), [transportation](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-transportation), and [energy](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-energy). So, climate impacts on these sectors can also affect the built environment.

Cities are taking steps to adapt to climate change. These actions are helping to protect the built environment, improve land-use planning, and increase emergency preparedness. For example, many cities are planting trees, installing green roofs, and adding green spaces. These measures help improve air quality and cool urban areas. More permeable ground cover also absorbs rainfall, reducing runoff and flooding. Some governments are using policy measures, like building codes and zoning laws, to protect the built environment and city residents from climate change.[5](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#5foot)

Urban Areas and Greenhouse Gases
--------------------------------

Even though cities only occupy about 1% to 5% of the land in North America, they produce 80 percent of all the greenhouse gases on the continent.[6](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#6foot) Read more about greenhouse gas emissions on the [Basics of Climate Change](https://www.epa.gov/climatechange-science/basics-climate-change) page.

Top Climate Impacts on the Built Environment
--------------------------------------------

Climate change has multiple effects on the built environment. Three key impacts are described in this section.

### 1. Damaged or Stressed Infrastructure

![Image 11: Crumbling bridge](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/small/private/images/2022-11/iStock-1060148592-v2.jpg?itok=u8zO0qMa)

Most of the U.S. built environment was not designed with climate change in mind and is vulnerable to climate change.[12](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#12foot)

A lot of U.S. infrastructure is nearing the end of its life. This aging infrastructure is threatened by extreme weather events—some of which are already becoming more frequent and/or more intense.[7](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#7foot)

Climate change impacts on infrastructure vary by region. For example, in coastal regions, high tide flooding has harmed or destroyed infrastructure and private property.[8](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#8foot) In the West, wildfires are already damaging property and affecting air quality.[9](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#9foot) In floodplains across the nation, extreme precipitation can cause dam failures, bridge washouts, and road closures.[10](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#10foot) Many cities also have outdated or aging sewer systems that intense rainfall can overwhelm, causing untreated wastewater to flow into local water bodies.[11](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#11foot)

Extreme temperatures can also stress the U.S. energy system.[13](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#13foot) Failing cooling systems can lead to high indoor temperatures that endanger [workers' health](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-and-health-workers)and reduce productivity.[14](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#14foot) Transportation systems are also vulnerable to multiple climate change stressors, including extreme temperatures and heavy precipitation.[15](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#15foot)Flooding from extreme precipitation and other climate impacts can cause billions of dollars of damage to transportation infrastructure annually.[16](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#16foot) Meanwhile, high temperatures can stress bridge integrity and weaken rail systems.[17](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#17foot)

### 2. Impacts on the Production and Distribution of Goods and Services

![Image 12: Flood damage to walkway](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/small/private/images/2022-11/iStock-852285366.jpg?itok=CIhnojId)

In Houston, 300,000 customers were without power when Hurricane Harvey struck in 2017. The storm also affected emergency services, hospitals, and water treatment plants.

Urban centers produce many goods that are important to the economy at local to global scales. Cities also provide many essential services to the public, such as telecommunications, energy, water, health care, and transportation.

A network of interconnected systems supports the flow of these goods and services. Climate change poses multiple and far-reaching risks to these systems.[18](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#18foot) For example, extreme weather can cause power outages that disrupt transportation, interrupt supply chains, limit communication, and make it difficult for people to access health care.

### 3. Threats to Quality of Life and Human Health

Cities provide access to jobs, education, recreation, arts, culture, and much more. But many cities already face challenges with aging infrastructure, social inequities, and other problems. Climate change can worsen these problems and pose additional risks. For example, climate impacts on [air quality](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-air-quality) and [water quality](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-freshwater-resources)can affect people’s health. Flooding can force people to evacuate their homes, affecting their mental and physical health. Sea level rise, flooding, and wildfires also threaten some historic landmarks, playgrounds, and cultural sites.

Forests, open spaces, and waterways in urban areas provide a wide range of benefits, but many are stressed due to development, pollution, and invasive species.[19](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#19foot) These existing challenges can reduce their resilience to climate change effects.[20](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#20foot)

For more specific examples of climate change impacts in your region, please see the [National Climate Assessment](https://nca2023.globalchange.gov/).

The Built Environment and the Economy
-------------------------------------

Urban areas are important engines of economic growth—hubs for producing and consuming goods and services. In 2015, urban areas contributed about 91% of the United States’ gross domestic product (GDP). The five largest cities in the country accounted for 23% of the GDP.[21](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#21foot) In addition, urban lands are worth trillions of dollars.[22](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#22foot)

However, cities also bear the burden of aging and declining infrastructure that needs major financial investments to replace or repair. Not addressing this problem could cost the U.S. GDP up to $3.9 trillion by 2025.[23](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#23foot) This estimate considers reduced goods production, lost trade, disrupted supply chains, fewer jobs, and lower wages. Services like transportation, water, electricity, and sanitation would also be affected, impacting people’s health and quality of life.

Population Impacts
------------------

![Image 13: Children at city playground](https://www.epa.gov/system/files/styles/small/private/images/2022-11/iStock-932956794.jpg?itok=rEDrXeHA)

Heat can affect children who spend time outdoors playing and exercising.

Some groups of urban residents are especially vulnerable to climate change impacts on the built environment. For example, [children](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-and-childrens-health), [older adults](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-and-health-older-adults), [outdoor workers](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-and-health-workers), and [people with some medical conditions](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-and-health-people-chronic-medical-conditions) are all sensitive to extreme heat.[24](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#24foot) The urban heat island effect in built-up areas will intensify the effects of extreme heat events and reduce the relief provided by cooler temperatures at night.[25](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#25foot) People who lack housing or who live in homes without adequate insulation or cooling are also more at risk for heat stress.[26](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#26foot)

In addition, many groups of urban residents live with high levels of air pollution.[27](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#27foot),[28](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#28foot) Black and African American individuals are 34% more likely to live in areas with the highest projected increases in childhood asthma due to climate-related changes in [particulate matter](https://www.epa.gov/pm-pollution).[29](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#29foot)

People who live in buildings that are older or in poor condition can be more exposed to outdoor pollutants and allergens.[30](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#30foot) These buildings may also sustain more damage during extreme weather, such as storms and floods. This can lead to damp indoor environments prone to mold and bacteria.[31](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#31foot)

Climate change can also affect food security in urban areas.[32](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#32foot) For example, power outages from extreme weather can cause food spoilage. Climate impacts on [agriculture](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-agriculture-and-food-supply) and [transportation](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-transportation) may affect supply chains, food prices, and availability.[33](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#33foot) People who already have limited access to food will be affected the most. These include households with an income below the poverty threshold, those headed by a single woman, and those with Black or Hispanic owners or lessees.[34](https://www.epa.gov/climateimpacts/climate-change-impacts-built-environment#34foot)

What We Can Do
--------------

We can reduce the impact of climate change on the built environment in many ways, including the following:

*   **Go for the green.** Green roofs, greenways, trees, and other [green infrastructure](https://www.epa.gov/green-infrastructure) all help reduce the [urban heat island effect](https://www.epa.gov/heatislands) and absorb rainwater to prevent flooding. They also [provide benefits](https://www.epa.gov/green-infrastructure/benefits-green-infrastructure) like improved air quality and habitat for wildlife.
*   **Create climate action plans.**City governments can [develop plans](https://www.epa.gov/smartgrowth/planning-framework-climate-resilient-economy) to identify vulnerabilities in the built environment and reduce the impacts of climate change.
*   **Build climate smart.**Planners should build with resilience in mind by considering projected climate changes and [smart growth strategies](https://www.epa.gov/smartgrowth/smart-growth-tools#Climate), as well as [planning for natural disasters](https://www.epa.gov/disaster-debris/guidance-about-planning-natural-disaster-debris) in advance.
*   **Engage with communities.** Cities can take action to reduce climate impacts on all citizens. For example, consider vulnerable populations when planning cooling centers, transportation routes, and green spaces.
*   **Get coordinated.** Climate responses often require coordination among many agencies and sectors. [EPA’s regional climate adaptation coordinators](https://www.epa.gov/arc-x/epa-regional-climate-adaptation-contacts-state-websites) can help provide guidance and resources.

See additional actions you can take, as well as steps that companies can take, on EPA’s [What You Can Do About Climate Change](https://www.epa.gov/climate-change/what-you-can-do-about-climate-change) page.

Highlights
----------

*   •Reviewed the links between precipitation trends and climate change dynamics. 
*   •Examined urban infrastructure susceptibility to changing climate conditions. 
*   •Explored innovative adaptive strategies worldwide for urban flood risk reduction. 
*   •Suggested future challenges and opportunities in addressing climate-induced urban flooding. 

Abstract
--------

Climate change is a serious global issue causing more extreme weather patterns, resulting in more frequent and severe events like urban flooding. This review explores the connection between climate change and urban flooding, offering statistical, scientific, and advanced perspectives. Analyses of precipitation patterns show clear changes, establishing a strong link between climate change and the heightened intensity of global rainfall events. [Hydrological modeling](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/hydrology-models "Learn more about Hydrological modeling from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and case studies provide compelling scientific evidence attributing urban flooding to climate-induced changes. Urban infrastructure, including transportation networks and critical facilities, is increasingly vulnerable, worsening the impact on people's lives and businesses. Examining adaptation strategies, the review highlights the need for resilient urban planning and the integration of [green infrastructure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/green-infrastructure "Learn more about green infrastructure from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). Additionally, it delves into the role of advanced technologies, such as artificial intelligence, [remote sensing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/remote-sensing "Learn more about remote sensing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and predictive modeling, in improving flood prediction, monitoring, and management. The socio-economic implications of urban flooding are discussed, emphasizing unequal vulnerability and the importance of inclusive policies. In conclusion, the review stresses the urgency of addressing climate-induced urban flooding through a holistic analysis of statistical trends, scientific evidence, infrastructure vulnerabilities, and adaptive measures. The integration of advanced technologies and a comprehensive understanding of socio-economic implications are essential for developing effective, inclusive strategies. This review serves as a valuable resource, offering insights for policymakers, researchers, and practitioners striving for climate-resilient urban futures amid escalating [climate change impacts](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/climate-change-impact "Learn more about climate change impacts from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages").

1. Introduction
---------------

Climate change poses a severe threat in the [Anthropocene](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/anthropocene "Learn more about Anthropocene from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") era, where human activities have significantly changed the Earth [[1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib1)]. It's altering our planet rapidly, especially evident in the increased occurrence of urban [flooding](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/flood "Learn more about flooding from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib2)]. This is a significant concern because of rising global temperatures, changes in rainfall patterns, and more extreme weather events [[3](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib3),[4](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib4)]. With their dense populations and extensive development, urban areas are particularly vulnerable to these changes. The modern trend of urbanization has transformed landscapes, creating large cities that now face a heightened risk of flooding due to these climate shifts. This rapid urban growth, coupled with changes in land use, has altered natural hydrological processes, reduced permeable surfaces and increased runoff [[[5]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib5), [[6]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib6), [[7]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib7)]. Consequently, urban areas are now more susceptible to flooding, provoked by the intensification of extreme weather events attributed to climate change [[8](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib8)]. The connection between climate change and urban flooding is a complex interaction of various factors that work together, each affecting and intensifying the other [[9](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib9),[10](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib10)]. Factors such as rapid urbanization, altered land-use patterns, and insufficient [stormwater management](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/stormwater-management "Learn more about stormwater management from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") infrastructure further complicate the complex connection between climate change and urban flooding [[11](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib11),[12](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib12)].

The primary concern of this issue is the impact of climate change on how and where precipitation occurs. The historical precipitation data specially analysed around Europe America and Canada, has shown a noticeable shift emerges in rainfall events' frequency, duration, and intensity [[13](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib13),[14](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib14)]. Statistical trends globally depict more extreme and unpredictable precipitation, causing some regions to endure prolonged droughts while others face severe floods [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib15)]. These changes in climate dynamics can occur due to human activities, particularly the release of greenhouse gases [[16](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib16)]. These altered precipitation patterns strongly affect urban areas, where insufficient drainage systems and non-absorbent surfaces increase the risk of flooding. For an instance, between 2000 and 2019, there were 548 recorded flood events in [Latin America](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/latin-america "Learn more about Latin America from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and the Caribbean. These events impacted a total of 41 million people and resulted in damages worth US$26 billion [[15](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib15)]. The increase in severe weather events, such as hurricanes, typhoons, and intense storms, further compounds the challenges for urban planners and policymakers.

Scientific rigor supports the claim that climate change is not a distant or hypothetical threat but an immediate and tangible reality shaping our environment [[17](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib17)]. Evidence from hydrological models, climate simulations, and empirical studies converges to strongly link climate change to the increased frequency and severity of urban flooding [[18](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib18),[19](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib19)]. The complex interaction of rising temperatures altered precipitation, and changes in sea levels contributes to the increased vulnerability of urban areas [[20](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib20),[21](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib21)]. Real-world examples from diverse [geographical regions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/geographical-region "Learn more about geographical regions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") further back this scientific explanation, illustrating instances where urban flooding can be directly attributed to climate-related shifts [[22](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib22),[23](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib23)]. Whether its coastal cities dealing with [rising sea levels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/rising-sea-level "Learn more about rising sea levels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") or inland urban centres experiencing sudden floods, these cases emphasize the need to prioritize climate change in comprehensive flood management strategies.

Climate change makes cities with their vast concrete landscapes more vulnerable. The aging infrastructure, like roads, sewage systems, [water supply](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/water-supply "Learn more about water supply from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and important facilities, needs to be better equipped to handle the challenges posed by climate shifts [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib24),[25](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib25)]. This highlights the urgent need for resilient urban planning. Transportation systems, often located in low-lying areas, are at risk of disruptions from flooding, leading to economic losses and difficulties in accessibility [[26](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib26)]. The outdated water supply and sewage systems, designed based on past climate patterns, need help to cope with the extremes of precipitation and drought that are now becoming more common. Critical facilities like hospitals and power plants are not immune to these challenges. Their vulnerabilities add extra risks to public safety and well-being. Cities must adapt and plan for these changing climate dynamics to ensure the safety and functionality of essential urban systems.

In response to the escalating risks posed by climate-induced urban flooding, a spectrum of adaptation strategies has emerged on local, regional, and global scales. Infrastructure modifications, including the construction of resilient buildings, improved drainage systems, and elevated transportation routes, form a fundamental layer of defence against the impacts of flooding [[[27]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib27), [[28]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib28), [[29]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib29), [[30]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib30)]. [Green infrastructure](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/green-infrastructure "Learn more about Green infrastructure from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), a holistic approach that integrates natural elements such as wetlands, green roofs, and permeable pavements into urban planning, has gained prominence [[31](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib31)]. These nature-based solutions mitigate flood risks and enhance urban aesthetics, biodiversity, and the overall quality of life. Community-based adaptation strategies recognize the role of local knowledge and community engagement in building resilience. Empowering communities to actively participate in flood management, disaster response, and long-term planning fosters a sense of ownership and strengthens the social structure in adversity [[32](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib32)].

In the recent decade, Artificial Intelligence (AI) and machine learning have played a key role by predicting floods based on various factors, allowing for early warnings and more efficient emergency responses [[33](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib33)]. [Remote sensing technologies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/remote-sensing-technology "Learn more about Remote sensing technologies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") constantly monitor real-time weather patterns, river levels, and land-use changes, providing a comprehensive understanding of urban flooding influences [[34](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib34),[35](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib35)]. These data and machine learning approaches support decision-making based on evidence and improve the accuracy of flood risk assessments. Using advanced computational tools, predictive modelling enables scenario analysis and simulations of potential flood situations. These models aid urban planners in creating infrastructure resilient to climate change challenges and guide policy decisions to reduce vulnerability [[24](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib24)]. This integration of technology offers a proactive and informed approach to managing and mitigating the impacts of floods in urban areas.

Urban flooding affects beyond physical infrastructure impact, causing significant socio-economic consequences [[36](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib36)]. Vulnerabilities are not evenly distributed, and marginalized communities often suffer the most [[37](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib37)]. Low-income neighbourhoods, usually located in flood-prone areas with insufficient infrastructure, experience heightened health risks, displacement, and economic losses [[38](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib38),[39](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib39)]. These socio-economic repercussions extend to the insurance and financial sectors. The rising frequency and severity of urban flooding challenge traditional risk assessment models, emphasizing the need for innovative financial instruments and insurance solutions that consider climate-induced risks [[40](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib40)]. This highlights the importance of collaboration across different sectors. Social justice plays a central role in discussions about urban flooding. Reasonable policies are crucial to address the disproportionate impact on vulnerable communities. Inclusive decision-making processes, community empowerment, and acknowledging diverse perspectives contribute to creating a more resilient and just urban landscape. The complexities of socio-economic considerations underscore the necessity of inclusive policies to tackle vulnerabilities and promote social justice.

2. Statistical trends in precipitation
--------------------------------------

### 2.1. Previous analysis of precipitation patterns

Studying past rainfall patterns is crucial for understanding how climate change affects our planet's water cycle. In the last century, records of the climate have become valuable sources of data, giving us insights into how rainfall is changing and helping us to identify the shifts in urban [flooding](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/flood "Learn more about flooding from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[41](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib41)]. In the past, rainfall used to follow certain predictable patterns, which allowed regions to plan their infrastructure and city layouts based on the expected climate conditions [[42](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib42),[43](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib43)]. However, with the rise of industrialization and the increased release of greenhouse gases, the climate has become less predictable.

Over the past few decades, an observable shift in the frequency, intensity, and distribution of precipitation events has become evident [[44](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib44),[45](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib45)]. Regions once familiar to predictable rainfall are experiencing more irregular and extreme weather phenomena [[[46]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib46), [[47]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib47), [[48]](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib48)]. Conversely, areas that historically faced arid conditions are witnessing unexpected attacks of heavy rainfall, challenging the existing water management strategies [[49](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib49),[50](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib50)]. Climate change, driven primarily by human activities such as burning [fossil fuels](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/fossil-fuel "Learn more about fossil fuels from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [deforestation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/deforestation "Learn more about deforestation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), has altered the [atmospheric composition](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/atmospheric-composition "Learn more about atmospheric composition from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") [[51](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib51),[52](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib52)]. This has led to a rise in global temperatures, triggering modifications in [atmospheric circulation](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/earth-and-planetary-sciences/atmospheric-circulation "Learn more about atmospheric circulation from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") patterns. Such alterations influence the movement of humidity-loaded air masses, resulting in changes to precipitation regimes. Over the last century, Europe has experienced more than 1500 weather events, such as droughts, floods, extreme temperature events and storms. The most frequently recorded natural disasters were floods (674 events), storms (569 events), and extreme temperature events (278 events) [[53](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib53)]. [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#fig1) illustrates a noticeable increase in flood events during the last decade (2013–2023), indicating a connection with climate change and extreme weather events.

In particular, urban areas bear witness to these shifts with heightened vulnerability. The historical data underscores an escalation in the frequency and intensity of precipitation events that surpass the design capacities of urban drainage systems [[54](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib54),[55](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib55)]. The outdated approach to urban planning, relying on past precipitation norms, proves insufficient in addressing the current changes [[56](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib56),[57](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib57)]. Moreover, the temporal dimension of historical precipitation analysis is vital. Patterns of change over decades or even centuries offer a comprehensive understanding of long-term trends, fostering in the identification of cyclical patterns or observing variances that may signify accelerated climate shifts [[58](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib58),[59](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib59)]. Taking time into account is crucial when developing strategies to adapt to changes in rainfall patterns, whether they happen slowly over time or suddenly shift.

### 2.2. Correlation between climate change and shifts in precipitation extremes

The connection between climate change and changes in extreme precipitation is crucial for understanding the evolving nature of urban flooding. Earth's climate is undergoing significant transformations, with the intensity and frequency of extreme precipitation events serving as critical indicators of these changes [[60](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib60),[61](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib61)]. Unlike earlier approaches focused on averages and long-term trends, recent decades have shifted toward examining extremes. This shift recognizes that the intensity of precipitation events is a primary driver of flooding [[62](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib62)]. Statistical analyses of precipitation extremes have exposed a clear correlation with global climate change, largely influenced by human activities [[63](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib63)].

The increase in global temperatures, driven by the buildup of greenhouse gases in the atmosphere, acts as a catalyst for disrupting the water cycle. Warmer air can hold more moisture, creating favourable conditions for intense rainfall events [[62](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib62),[64](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib64)]. This fundamental thermodynamic principle forms the basis for understanding the connection between climate change and observed shifts in precipitation extremes [[65](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib65)]. Global observational data consistently demonstrate a rise in the frequency and intensity of heavy precipitation events [[66](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib66)]. The link between escalating global temperatures and extreme precipitation is highlighted by a significant fact: for every 1°C increase in temperature, the atmosphere can hold about 7% more water vapor [[67](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2590123024003773#bib67),[68](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S259

# Tracking urban flood exposure: Global trends since 1985

![River-side settlement expansion in Qu?ng Nam, Vietnam, 2002 (left) and 2021 (right)](https://s7d1.scene7.com/is/image/wbcollab/blog-rapid-urban-growth_1?qlt=90&fmt=webp&resMode=sharp2 "River-side settlement expansion in Qu?ng Nam, Vietnam, 2002 (left) and 2021 (right)")

The world’s cities are growing rapidly as people move in search of economic opportunities. [Urbanization and economic development have traditionally gone hand in hand as cities attract people, ideas, and capital, and foster agglomeration economies.](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=Urbanization+and+economic+development+have+traditionally+gone+hand+in+hand+as+cities+attract+people%2C+ideas%2C+and+capital%2C+and+foster+agglomeration+economies.&url=https://blogs.worldbank.org/en/sustainablecities/tracking-urban-flood-exposure-global-trends-1985?cid=SHR_BlogSiteTweetable_EN_EXT&via=@worldbank) However, rapid urbanization can also cause congestion effects; for instance, by increasing exposure to natural hazards and pressure on public services and infrastructure.

[The effect of rapid urbanization on flood risks and people’s vulnerability is an intensifying challenge for low and middle-income countries.](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=The+effect+of+rapid+urbanization+on+flood+risks+and+people%E2%80%99s+vulnerability+is+an+intensifying+challenge+for+low+and+middle-income+countries.&url=https://blogs.worldbank.org/en/sustainablecities/tracking-urban-flood-exposure-global-trends-1985?cid=SHR_BlogSiteTweetable_EN_EXT&via=@worldbank) This is particularly relevant in countries that lack the capacity for risk-informed urban and infrastructure planning and the resources to invest in protection infrastructure. And as safe spaces for construction are increasingly occupied, high population density and land scarcity are driving new developments disproportionately into previously avoided areas, including riverbeds and flood plains.

In [a study just published in the journal Nature](https://www.nature.com/articles/s41586-023-06468-9), and financially supported by the [Global Facility for Disaster Reduction and Recovery (GFDRR)](https://www.gfdrr.org/en), we provide systematic evidence that communities around the world – from rural villages to large cities – have been continuously and rapidly expanding into flood zones. [Despite the evidence that climate change will make extreme precipitation and floods more frequent in many regions, many countries continue to increase their exposure to such threats with unplanned urban expansion.](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=Despite+the+evidence+that+climate+change+will+make+extreme+precipitation+and+floods+more+frequent+in+many+regions%2C+many+countries+continue+to+increase+their+exposure+to+such+threats+with+unplanned+urban+expansion.&url=https://blogs.worldbank.org/en/sustainablecities/tracking-urban-flood-exposure-global-trends-1985?cid=SHR_BlogSiteTweetable_EN_EXT&via=@worldbank)

### Most rapid high-risk growth in East Asia, and upper-middle-income countries

[In many regions, growth in the most hazardous flood zones has been outpacing growth in safe zones by a large margin.](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=In+many+regions%2C+growth+in+the+most+hazardous+flood+zones+has+been+outpacing+growth+in+safe+zones+by+a+large+margin.&url=https://blogs.worldbank.org/en/sustainablecities/tracking-urban-flood-exposure-global-trends-1985?cid=SHR_BlogSiteTweetable_EN_EXT&via=@worldbank) This is particularly true in East Asia, where high-hazard settlements have expanded 60% faster than flood-safe ones. More generally, this is true in middle-income countries (MIC), where economies and cities have grown rapidly over the past decades. The effects are most visible in upper-middle-income countries, which have a higher proportion of settlements in the highest-hazard areas than any other group. Since 1985, these have grown by 184% —nearly twice the rate of flood-safe settlements (96%). These expanding settlements in high-hazard areas lock in flood exposure, as well as future losses and the need for ever-mounting flood protection investments.

**Settlement growth (%) by hazard level**

![Settlement growth (%) by hazard level](https://s7d1.scene7.com/is/image/wbcollab/urban-growth5?qlt=90&fmt=webp&resMode=sharp2)

### Slow and safe urban growth in low-income countries so far

Compared with MICs, low-income countries (LIC) have seen moderate settlement growth since 1985, and this growth is mostly occurring on safe land: on average, settlements in high-hazard areas have grown 77%, compared to 87% in safe areas. The slower urbanization in these countries is likely to create less pressure to use all available land, including the riskiest one.

These trends are likely to change in the future. As today’s LICs follow on a path of economic growth, urbanization rates are likely to accelerate, which is conducive to faster development. But without risk-informed urban planning and appropriate [zoning policies](https://openknowledge.worldbank.org/server/api/core/bitstreams/ac483417-6a6f-53a6-ac1b-5c3b70eccfa1/content), climatic risk exposure in low-income urban hubs is likely to increase in the future.

### What it means for urban planning and climate resilience

It can be politically convenient to see climate change as an exogenous force that places disasters beyond the influence of local authorities. However, locally determined patterns of urbanization and spatial development are key factors in people’s exposure and vulnerability to climatic shocks.

Subnationally disaggregated results show a pattern of divergence in exposure to flood hazard, with flood-safe growth dominating in some locations, while others are increasing their relative exposure to floods. This highlights a key responsibility that falls on municipalities and subnational governments, who often are charged with land-use and urbanization plans and regulations (including their enforcement).

**Flood-safe vs flood-exposed growth: Relative difference between settlement growth in safe and high hazard areas 1985 to 2015**

![Flood-safe vs flood-exposed growth: Relative difference between settlement growth in safe and high hazard areas 1985 to 2015 ](https://s7d1.scene7.com/is/image/wbcollab/urban-growth4?qlt=90&fmt=webp&resMode=sharp2)

In an age of increasing disaster losses due to flood events, effective flood risk mitigation efforts must be based on a robust understanding of the different contributing factors to flood risk. These can include intensifying hazards (e.g., due to climate change), increasing exposure to hazards (due to expanding at-risk settlements), and rising vulnerability to shocks (e.g., due to inadequate building standards or social protection systems). Using data with high spatiotemporal resolution, our study shows that rising exposure is key to understanding the global rise in flood disaster losses – [continuous monitoring of urbanization and flood exposure patterns can help guide timely interventions.](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=continuous+monitoring+of+urbanization+and+flood+exposure+patterns+can+help+guide+timely+interventions.&url=https://blogs.worldbank.org/en/sustainablecities/tracking-urban-flood-exposure-global-trends-1985?cid=SHR_BlogSiteTweetable_EN_EXT&via=@worldbank)

![River-side settlement expansion in Cap-Haïtien, Haiti, 2010 (left) and 2021 (right)](https://s7d1.scene7.com/is/image/wbcollab/blog-rapid-urban-growth2?qlt=90&fmt=webp&resMode=sharp2)

These findings have concrete implications for urban planners and policy makers. [In areas where flood exposure is already high, investments in disaster preparedness and protection are crucial to mitigate losses](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=In+areas+where+flood+exposure+is+already+high%2C+investments+in+disaster+preparedness+and+protection+are+crucial+to+mitigate+losses&url=https://blogs.worldbank.org/en/sustainablecities/tracking-urban-flood-exposure-global-trends-1985?cid=SHR_BlogSiteTweetable_EN_EXT&via=@worldbank) (e.g. early warning systems, social protection, and protective infrastructure). [In areas where flood exposure is still low but rapidly increasing, revision of land use and urbanization plans are urgent priorities to avoid new construction in the highest-risk areas](https://twitter.com/intent/tweet?text=In+areas+where+flood+exposure+is+still+low+but+rapidly+increasing%2C+revision+of+land+use+and+urbanization+plans+are+urgent+priorities+to+avoid+new+construction+in+the+highest-risk+areas&url=https://blogs.worldbank.org/en/sustainablecities/tracking-urban-flood-exposure-global-trends-1985?cid=SHR_BlogSiteTweetable_EN_EXT&via=@worldbank): once an area is urbanized, it is impossible or prohibitively expensive to reverse it, thus locking-in high-risk patterns with large economic and human consequences. While land scarcity and geographic constraints can mean that flood zones cannot always be avoided, careful planning of protection systems and disaster preparedness can support resilient development.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Urban areas face heightened flood risks due to dense populations, aging infrastructure, and inadequate drainage systems, exacerbated by climate change and extreme weather events.
Climate change intensifies weather events, leading to more frequent and severe urban flooding, as demonstrated by changing precipitation patterns and rising sea levels.
Rapid urbanization contributes significantly to flood vulnerability, as cities expand into flood-prone areas without sufficient infrastructure or effective urban planning to manage stormwater.
Effective drainage systems are essential to manage increased rainfall and runoff, preventing urban flooding and protecting infrastructure and communities from water-related disasters.
Ensuring public safety in urban areas requires proactive flood management strategies, including community awareness and resilience planning to protect vulnerable populations from flooding impacts.
